text: a stubborn ass. How I went from never wanting children to caring about a child so much I’m willingly choosing to suffer through natural childbirth is wild. But this little goat turd healed me in so many ways. It seems only right I do the best I can by her. Come what may. We finally pull up in front of the Boulder Medical Center, where Wyatt, Calder, and Luke all damn near carry me out of the truck to place me gently into the wheelchair a nurse is holding for us. “Which one is the dad?” she asks, and Wyatt’s hand instantly shoots up while Calder and Luke stand back awkwardly. “Those two are the uncles,” I state proudly and feel my eyes sting with emotion as they both smirk proudly. “We’ll see you guys on the other side, okay?” They nod gratefully, and the nurse wheels me away with my mountain man and two of the best brothers a girl could ask for waiting in the wings. “Baby, open your eyes.” Wyatt’s deep voice breaks through the intense mental fog that has overtaken me the past forty minutes. “She’s out!” “What?” I croak and my voice sounds weird...like I’ve been screaming, but I don’t remember making any sounds. Honestly, I blacked out, I think. Like, I remember the searing pain, and I remember staring into nothing and making up a shape for each contraction, but once they put me in the stirrups and had me start pushing, I went into autopilot. It felt similar to vomiting, where my whole body just sort of took over and there was nothing I could do but just let it do its thing. If I’m being truthful, I knew I was laboring all day long on the mountain. The cramps I was feeling were different than Braxton-Hicks, but moving made them more tolerable, and I had shit to get done, so labor had to wait. The water-breaking thing just caught me off guard. “It’s a girl,” the doctor states grandly, and I have to blink my eyes multiple times to focus on the image between my legs. They fling the bony little thing up onto my chest, and I sort of stare at her on top of me, feeling bewildered and confused. The nurses aggressively wipe the goo off her, and I frown as I look to the doctor. “Is she okay?” “Yes! She’s perfect,” the doctor says on a laugh. I nod. Good, that’s good, and then my eyes find hers again. “Is my vagina okay?” She glances down between my legs. “Yep, it’s perfect too.” “Well, I know that’s a lie,” I harrumph, and faintly hear Wyatt and the nurses chuckling at me while they help him cut the umbilical cord and wrap the baby up in a tight little bundle. Finally, I pry my hands off the bed rails. I don’t know what they were doing there still. Frozen in terror, I guess? All I know is my fingers ache, so I stretch them out before I finally let myself hold my baby. My baby. She cries loudly, and on autopilot once again, I shush her before leaning down to coo softly into her ear. “There, there, baby girl. I’ve got you. Mommy’s got you.” She instantly stops crying, her little body soothed by the sound of my voice, and this strange sense of confidence overcomes me. I feel utterly certain of what I am supposed to do with this little one. She isn’t just my baby. This is my soul mate. I already know her. I’ve known her my whole life. And this moment, this magical moment, was mine from the beginning of time. “I’m sorry for the rocky journey to get here,” I whisper into her soft, delicate skin. “But you sure as heck were worth it.” I kiss her soft cheek, and a complete sense of peace and calm descends on me. A strange noise echoes above me, and I look up to find Wyatt full-on sobbing. His face is twisted up in a weird way, and I resign myself to the fact that this man is not an attractive crier. But my God, he’s beautiful. I reach up and wipe a tear off his cheek, pulling him in close. His big brawny arms wrap around me and our little girl, the warmth causing the nurses, the doctor, and whatever ungodly thing she’s doing to my “perfect” vagina to all melt away. It’s just me, my baby, my mountain man, and a happy ending beyond all my wildest dreams. Copyright © 2024 by Amy Daws Bonus Epilogue Animals on the Mountain: 33 Babies on the Mountain: 2 Wyatt Five Years Later “Hello, this is Mount Millie Rescue Center. Can I help you?” Trista answers her cell phone with a nine-month-old dangling from her body in one of those baby-carrier wrap things that I can never figure out. She drops the can of grain into Millie’s trough while listening intently on the phone. Millie barrels in beside her, devouring the feed like she hasn’t eaten in weeks. “Reindeer? Seriously? Uh-huh. How many heads?” She nods, and our son begins babbling loudly beneath her, so she sets the can down to mindlessly pat his butt. “Nine? Oh my God, is Santa coming too?” She laughs at whoever is on the phone and then nods. “No, we can handle them. I’ll need a couple of days to run some fencing, though. Can they hold them for me? Okay... I’ll let you know when we’re ready.” As soon as she hangs up the phone, she squeals, jumping up and down, to our son’s delight. “Did you hear that, Dax? We’re getting Santa’s reindeer!” Dax babbles nonsense, and Trista frowns and looks around. “Where’s Stevie? She’ll have a more appropriate reaction.” Her eyes find me watching her from the steps that lead to the barn apartment. Or I should say former barn apartment. It has been converted into the Mount Millie’s Rescue
